This chapter introduces the concept of digital religion, which asserts that to understand religion in contemporary culture, we must recognize that people engage in communal spiritual practices in both online-mediated spaces as well as through traditional offline communities and experiences. Those who study how religion takes place via Internet platforms have been particularly interested in how involvement in online communities may inform members’ understanding of what it means to be part of a religious group or church, especially how this may alter future expectations of how religious communities understand their faith offline (Hutchings, 2011. Working through these challenges and experimenting with how Internet technologies could provide solutions to the social separation encountered by their communities, religious leaders produced dozens of new expressions of religious practice, from digital worship services to online religious education, digital spiritual mentoring and counseling, Zoom meditation, and prayer gatherings.

## Abstract

This chapter introduces the concept of digital religion, which asserts that to understand religion in contemporary culture, we must recognize that people engage in communal spiritual practices in both online-mediated spaces as well as through traditional offline communities and experiences. I argue that our online and offline experiences of religion are deeply interconnected and force us to re-examine the nature of human spirituality in the twenty-first century. In this chapter, I discuss and define three key traits of digital religion: networked community, convergent practice, and multisite reality. Each of these traits suggests that the ways people participate in religious communities, rituals, and spiritual spaces have been altered by our engagement in a culture that is increasingly shaped by digital technologies. These three characteristics of digital religion became especially evident for religious groups during the global COVID-19 pandemic. They forced many congregations to move their religious services and practice online due to the social limits created by this health crisis. I assert that these new understandings about how individuals gather, practice their faith, and form spiritual relationships represent a unique and important shift in how religion should be conceived in Western culture.

## Introduction

As someone who has studied how religious groups have used and responded to Internet technologies since the 1990s, the global COVID-19 pandemic proved to be a pivotal time period for religious congregations adopting and adapting to digital technologies. I clearly remember logging onto Facebook on the first Sunday of the lockdown in the United States in March 2020. For the prior decade, I would often log on to Facebook on a Sunday morning and see about a half dozen friends and contacts streaming religious services they ran or attended on their Facebook profiles, inviting others to watch or worship with them. But that morning, my feed overflowed with over 30 live-streamed services running concurrently. One showed a large screenshot of a single Orthodox icon with the sound of a priest’s lone voice singing over it. A Lutheran priest stood alone in front of an altar of his church in Sweden leading prayers. A Pentecostal pastor in Mexico used a camera at the back of their church to try and capture their worship team, who were spread throughout their sanctuary, and an Episcopal pastor in the United States sat uncomfortably in his home study talking to his invisible congregation.

For the next 3 months, I tuned in each Sunday to observe the number of live-streamed religious services increase, and so I began to document what I have referred to in many talks on the impact of the pandemic on religion as the forced digital transition of religious congregations. The global COVID-19 pandemic left many religious leaders worldwide with only one option: either be willing to experiment with technology to keep their services going or lose connection with their congregations. Many pastors and priests also quickly discovered their congregations were victims of a digital divide, encountering a notable gap in their access to digital technologies and Internet access (Campbell, [2023](#ref-CR9 "Campbell, H. A. (2023). When churches discovered the digital divide: Overcoming technological inaccessibility, hesitancy & digital reluctance during the COVID-19 pandemic. Ecclesial Practices, 10(1), 36–61.")). It was not uncommon for many smaller churches to have no Wi-Fi set up in their buildings, let alone access to computers or digitally compatible audio equipment. March through May 2020 ushered in a large learning curve for religious congregations about how to best use and invest in information communication technology. Many churches had to rely on borrowed laptops, digital cameras, and even smartphones in those early days to try and figure out the best ways to build and connect to members of their congregation.

The forced digital transition of these congregations and other groups over the COVID-19 pandemic resulted in a huge uptake in digital media by religious communities as they tried to figure out the best ways to meet basic needs so they could continue their communication, programs, and connections during challenging times of social separation (Campbell & Osteen, [2021](#ref-CR11 "Campbell, H. A., & Osteen, S. (2021). When pastors put on the “tech hat”: How churches digitized during COVID-19. 
                  https://oaktrust.library.tamu.edu/handle/1969.1/194959
                  
                "); Holleman et al., [2022](#ref-CR17 "Holleman, A., Roso, J., & Chaves, M. (2022). Religious congregations’ technological and financial capacities on the eve of the COVID-19 pandemic. Review of Religious Research, 64, 163–188.")). In many respects, it was as if churches and other religious groups discovered the potential of Internet technologies to serve religious communities for the first time. This created a collective ah-ha moment for many religious leaders about how technology could empower the communal outreach and spiritual development of twenty-first-century congregations. While leaders sought to learn about and adopt technology quickly, they also realized they needed to quickly catch up on understanding a digital culture that had grown up around them, a culture that they had either not been willing to acknowledge or recognize. The pandemic did not create a dependency on technology. Still, in many respects, it did highlight how digital media has become a crucial part of our work, education, politics, and even family life (Bryson et al., [2020](#ref-CR5 "Bryson, J. R., Andres, L., & Davies, A. (2020). COVID-19, virtual church services and a new temporary geography of home. Tijdschrift voor economische en sociale geografie, 111(3), 360–372."); Chow & Kurlberg, [2020](#ref-CR14 "Chow, A., & Kurlberg, J. (2020). Two or three gathered online: Asian and European responses to COVID-19 and the digital church. Studies in World Christianity, 26(3), 298–318."); Dunlow, [2021](#ref-CR15 "Dunlow, J. (2021). Digital discipleship: A study of how churches in New York used technology for adult discipleship during the COVID-19 pandemic. Christian Education Journal, 18(3), 458–472.")). If this is the case, why not welcome it or at least consider bringing it into our religious lives?

Indeed, the use of digital media by religious groups and individuals to facilitate religious practices is in no way new or unique to the pandemic experience. In fact, over 25 years of research has been collected documenting how religious groups experiment with technology for spiritual purposes (see Campbell, [2013](#ref-CR8 "Campbell, H. A. (2013). Digital religion: Understanding religious practice in new media worlds. Routledge."); Campbell & Tsuria, [2021](#ref-CR12 "Campbell, H. A., & Tsuria, R. (2021). Digital religion: Understanding religious practice in digital culture (2nd ed.). Routledge.")). Yet, unique to the pandemic era was the wide-scale adoption and conscious integration of digital media into the life and work of many religious communities around the world. But rather than seeing this as a time of crisis that encouraged religious groups to innovate or cease to function for a season, I see the COVID-19 pandemic as a time of compulsory and unavoidable change many religious communities had been consciously avoiding. This moment required religious congregations to face the realities of life in the twenty-first century, a world that had already been wrapped in and driven by digital technology for many years. The pandemic forced religious communities to recognize our society, and religious communities are increasingly affected by digital culture (Lipková & Jarolímková, [2023](#ref-CR20 "Lipková, H., & Jarolímková, A. (2023). Twist in perception: Spiritual needs and technology in the times of COVID-19: A qualitative research study in the Czech Republic. Pastoral Psychology, 72(1), 143–167."); Ugboh, [2023](#ref-CR23 "Ugboh, G. (2023). The church and techno-theology: A paradigm shift of theology and theological practice to overcome technological disruptions. Journal of Ethics in Entrepreneurship and Technology, 3, 59–78.")).

## A World of Digital Religion

Living in a world that has been and continues to be shaped by digital media use requires religious communities to open their eyes and see how technology is, directly and indirectly, influencing the social practices and outlook of people in their congregations. Even if religious communities sought to avoid or ignore technology before the pandemic, it still informed many aspects of how their members communicated, worked, and developed spiritual disciplines in their everyday lives. Researchers like me have been documenting and investigating these trends for over two decades. Many aspects of digital technology shaping our current culture could be explored in this chapter, such as how digital practices have informed how we understand authority, identity, and authenticity in both secular and religious contexts (see Campbell & Bellar, [2023](#ref-CR10 "Campbell, H., & Bellar, W. (2023). Digital religion: The basics. Routledge.")). However, in order to focus the argument of this chapter, I limit my discussion to three prominent ways our spiritual practices and patterns have come to be informed by the digital tools and spaces in which we regularly interact. The social conditions and new form of interaction required during the COVID-19 pandemic spotlighted important ways that living in a digital culture has altered our understanding of religious community, ritual, and place. While these traits were made evident for most religious communities through their mediated experiences during the pandemic, they are in no way unique to the pandemic. I identified these tendencies over a decade ago in a research article published in *the Journal of the American Academy of Religion* and formed the basis of digital religion (Campbell, [2013](#ref-CR8 "Campbell, H. A. (2013). Digital religion: Understanding religious practice in new media worlds. Routledge.")).

Digital religion is a new conception of religion, which argues that in order to understand how religion is perceived and practiced in the twenty-first century, we must consider how offline or in-person religious practices intersect and are connected to online or digital religious practices. This is an understanding of religion, which I have identified and developed to better explain what contemporary religious practice looks like as it is engaged in both online and offline spaces. I argue that our online and offline experiences of religion are deeply interconnected, and our digital religious practices have come to inform our broader understanding of religion in society. In this chapter, I define and discuss the traits of networked community, convergent practice, and multisite reality and show how these characteristics of digital religion could be seen at work both before and during the COVID-19 pandemic.

## Networked Community

I argued that central to the idea of digital religion is the concept of networked community. This trait of networked community highlights the fact people increasingly build their social relationships in and through digital means. This form of network-based community building, in many ways, deviates from traditional forms of community. Now, we often create our social relationships through online spaces, such as forming a friendship around shared interests via an Instagram account or using a Facebook group to gather people who share a similar passion. So how people learn to *see* or interact with one another, and their social patterns become informed through the structures of networked technology (Wellman, [2001](#ref-CR24 "Wellman, B. (2001). Computer networks as social networks. Science, 293(5537), 2031–2034.")). Internet technology facilitates the building of what is referred to as networked interactions, social connections built through patterns of internetworking that privilege personal relational preferences over communal needs (Wellman, [2018](#ref-CR25 "Wellman, B. (2018). The network community: An introduction. In B. Wellman (Ed.), Networks in the global village (pp. 1–47). Routledge.")).

Since the early 1990s, researchers have been studying how people form and maintain relationships through Internet technology, which has come to be referred to as online communities. Scholars have shown that involvement in Internet-based groups often shapes their members’ broader conception of and participation in their offline communities. Those who study how religion takes place via Internet platforms have been particularly interested in how involvement in online communities may inform members’ understanding of what it means to be part of a religious group or church, especially how this may alter future expectations of how religious communities understand their faith offline (Hutchings, [2011](#ref-CR19 "Hutchings, T. (2011). Contemporary religious community and the online church. Information, Communication & Society, 14(8), 1118–1135.")). This is because online communities function quite differently than traditional religious institutions and structures. Rather than operating as tightly bounded groups, where social relationships are based on strong family ties, the limits of geography, and historic institutional affiliations, the online community is based on social connections driven by individual preferences and choices. This creates varying levels of affiliation and commitment. These loose social networks can be molded to the needs of individuals over groups. This concept of networked community suggests a fundamental change in how community is formed and maintained within online contexts, which has spilled over into how individuals conceive of community offline as well.

In the late 1990s, researchers began investigating this shifting understanding of the community they saw emerging within many secular and religious online groups. Early religious communities online involved people creating connections via email, discussion forums, or websites. For example, Muslims formed online communities to discuss issues of religious law via online fatwa websites. Buddhists developed new ways to conduct religious study or debate via cybersangha forums. Also, Jewish groups experimented with using early virtual reality (VR) and web space to create digital synagogues and prayer *minyans*. The Internet also allowed many spiritual teachers to offer classes and training, such as those offered by Spirituality & Practice, Center for Action and Contemplation, and later, many others. Scholars like me were interested in exploring individuals’ motivation for joining and investing time in these online communities. My early studies of online Christian communities showed that these groups did not see themselves as unconnected from offline Christianity, even though the traditional practices of prayer, worship, or spiritual development often needed to be modified when imported online.

Religious leaders have often voiced fear that involvement in an online religious community would undermine commitments to offline religious institutions and churches. Yet, studies like mine consistently demonstrated that online community typically served as a supplement, not substitute for offline religious involvement. Online religious community members often joined these online communities in order to meet specific relational needs, yet many recognized that these groups could not fully meet social needs and a desire for shared embodied worship experience. The new social structures and interactions that emerged within these online groups also require a new understanding of how contemporary religious groups function. It also highlighted the tendency for individuals to function within multiple social groups simultaneously, both online and offline.

These trends point toward a networked understanding of community, where people create specialized connections with groups that fit in with their spiritual needs and preferences, moving between involvement in different social groups so that they create a unique personalized network of relations. Online communities provide a space to easily build such personal social connections with others online and still affiliate with a broader or offline religious community. For many people during the pandemic, the idea of their social community being a network of self-directed relationships mediated via technology became a lived reality. Individuals could easily visit or become involved in multiple religious communities. For example, they may log on to an online platform like Second Life or the metaverse to attend an online Christian worship service and, afterward, find a website where they could practice meditation with another group through the use of avatars. This potential for creating individualized yet communal experiences online also expresses a networked form of community where the members can choose the depth of their involvement and affiliation.

Seeing and understanding a religious community as a social network is not limited to studies of religion online. Since the late 1990s, sociologists of religion have begun to note that offline religious communities increasingly employ social networking practices as they studied the patterns of some religious organizations. For example, Ammerman’s ([1997](#ref-CR2 "Ammerman, N. (1997). Congregation and community. Rutgers University Press.")) study of religious congregations saw many churches “functioning as a network” (p. 346). It asserted that “understanding of the social systems of modern life must start with the individual’s network of relations,” and thus, congregations should be approached as part of the social network of a community (p. 352). Using a social network analysis approach has also been noted in a number of studies of religious institutions and groups to assess levels of religious investment or degrees of social capital formed within these groups (Smith, [2003](#ref-CR22 "Smith, C. (2003). The secular revolution. University of California Press.")). For the past two decades, I have argued that the network metaphor provides a more accurate description of contemporary patterns of relationships, both online and offline. The network metaphor has become important for describing how people build, connect, and maintain their valued social relations online and offline, which are changeable, varying in depth, self-directed, and highly personalized. Community as a network provides an important new narrative for thinking about our religious interactions and spiritual relationships, especially coming out of the COVID-19 pandemic experience.

## Convergent Practice

Convergent practice emphasizes the fact that in the twenty-first century, many people now live out their spirituality by engaging in both online and offline forms of religious ritual, combining them into a personalized pattern of spirituality. Scholars of religion online have closely studied how spiritual traditions and religious rituals often must be adapted to fit within the technological structures and constraints of Internet platforms. For example, many spiritual practitioners and groups have had to think long and hard about how to transfer different embodied practices into disembodied digital spaces. Can an avatar performing prayer in an online temple in the VR space, such as the metaverse, be seen as an acceptable form of religious worship? Does gathering on a virtual meeting platform like Zoom for religious services count as a satisfactory way of performing one’s regular devotional practices? The debate about the authenticity or legitimacy of doing religious rituals online came to a head during the pandemic, with many communities deeming some forms of gathering online as an acceptable substitute, at least for a time. In contrast, other forms were deemed as not, such as the strong debates within Catholic and Orthodox communities about the theological limits of online Communion practices.

One of the other innate challenges posed by transferring religious practice online is that they were taken out of the communal, public context in which they were normally performed. They were taken out of an arena where they had a clear oversight of a recognized religious leader and placed within a personal and private context. Instead of sitting among one’s faith community and under the scrutiny of leaders during religious worship, the pandemic brought worship into private homes. This bringing of the sacred practice into mundane space meant the ritual was decontextualized, and for many, that meant it could be influenced by the culture of the home and the shape of technology. The trend of doing worship services online spawned hundreds of memes. Often depicted were families sitting on their living room couches watching worship services, usually in their pajamas or with a coffee in hand, turning this holy, active event into a passive spectacle. (For a discussion on the sacred, see Pargament and Pomerleau’s Chap. [4](https://doi.org/10.1007/978-3-031-93436-0_4) in this book.)

Yet even before the pandemic, research has noted that the culture of Internet searching and hyperlinks has already impacted how we seek information and conceive of facts and knowledge online. The Internet has made vast amounts of religious information accessible to community members and has taken private knowledge accessible only to religious community leaders and made it public. One of the byproducts of that is the flexibility of religious rituals online and fluid access to multiple sources of religious material can encourage individuals to blend aspects of rites based on information from new sources or experiences in different religious contexts. It can be argued that just like a networked community facilitates individualized forms of community, Internet platforms enable the faithful to build their own self-directed form of spiritual engagement online. Here, the Internet serves as a spiritual hub, allowing spiritual seekers to select from a vast array of resources and experiences to assemble and personalize their religious behavior and beliefs. This encourages a convergent form of religious practice online. Convergence is a term used to describe how digital media have brought together and combined traditional media technologies into a new form and in a new way. Digital media is a combination of telecommunication, broadcasting, and computer technologies linked through the digitization process. Scholars have also asserted that this technological convergence, seen clearly in the form and function of the Internet, is mirrored by a cultural convergence taking place in digital culture. The cultural convergence of digital media is that media audiences are no longer just consumers of content. Still, digital tools allow them to become cocreators of digital images and stories. This reshaping of the audience into a consumer and producer at the same time facilitates a process that allows and even encourages them to draw from multiple sources simultaneously. So, the technological landscape of the Internet removes many traditional barriers, allowing religious users new levels of freedom to create, access, and combine spiritual resources and practices that fit their own religious interpretations and experiences.

This increases the possibilities for how people interpret and express their religious lives online. For many, this means greater exposure to a myriad of spiritual beliefs, forms, and interpretations than they would otherwise have access to, both from within and without their religious tradition. This creates the possibility of creating new hybrids of traditional religions as different forms of knowledge and practice are combined to create individualized patterns of spiritual life. The Internet thus becomes, for many, a religious marketplace encouraging users to seek out preferred information or establish personalized practices rather than simply link to an official religious institution or mirror set protocols like they might have done in the past.

This tendency for religious Internet users to engage in convergent practices has been well documented. Initial investigations found early religious adopters of the Internet readily embraced the Internet as a sacred space in order to both import traditional rituals online and create new forms of religiosity online. Researchers have documented the adoption and adaptation of various religious forms in online environments, such as Hindus and Buddhists engaging in chanting and meditation in front of specially designed cyber-altars or shrines online meant to replicate holy sites from their country of origin so they can take place in virtual ceremonies during religious festivals. Helland ([2008](#ref-CR16 "Helland, C. (2008). Online religion as lived religion: Methodological issues in the study of religious participation on the internet. Heidelberg Journal of Religions on the Internet. 
                  https://doi.org/10.11588/rel.2005.1.380
                  
                ")) argued the very structure of the Internet supports and even encourages the emergence of diverse religious traditions. Importing traditional religious rituals and artifacts online means religious seekers have the opportunity to reinterpret the context and use, thereby transforming traditional practice in ways that may challenge offline ecclesiastical organizations. The Internet allows many religious devotees access to information and sources of interpretation that were once exclusively accessible to learned or high-ranking religious leaders. This can allow members to bypass traditional gatekeepers of religious knowledge (Barker, [2005](#ref-CR3 "Barker, E. (2005). Crossing the boundary: New challenges to religious authority and control as a consequence of access to the internet. In M. T. Hojsgaard & M. Warburg (Eds.), Religion and cyberspace (pp. 67–85). Routledge.")).

The fluidity of hypertexts and hyperlinks also creates new possibilities for spiritual pilgrimage and linking different ideas and concepts. As the Internet empowers religious seekers with a certain degree of autonomy and as more religious information becomes available online, it facilitates new freedom in the acquisition of ritual knowledge that may bypass normally required initiation rites. This encourages the personal pursuit of religious knowledge that will benefit the chosen life practice of the individual religious practitioner. Because this religious practice is taking place within an online religious group, it may not be so much about the collective performance of a set of rituals but about the freedom of collective reinterpretation. In this way, an online group can become constituted as a conversational community, allowing individuals to practice their faith in very individualized ways instead of being tied to the boundaries or constraints of a specific religious tradition. Therefore, through sharing new ideas and reinterpretations of symbols related to religious practice, an experiential environment is created, allowing the group to be together yet separately live out their spiritualities. Revista Românească pentru Educaţie Multidimensională, 17(2), 302-343. https://doi.org/10.18662/rrem/17.2/984 Revista Românească pentru Educaţie Multidimensională April-June 2025 Volume 17, Issue 2 303 Introduction The advent of digital platforms and the widespread availability of religious content on the Internet have led to profound transformations in religious education and practice (Ergen, 2023). Traditionally, religious knowledge was primarily controlled by institutional authorities, such as the Church, which monopolized access to sacred texts, religious artifacts, and theological interpretation (Wohlrab-Sahr et al., 2024). Participation in religious life necessitated physical presence in places of worship and direct engagement with clerical authorities. However, with the rise of digital technology, these historical dynamics have undergone significant changes, reshaping the epistemology of religious knowledge and the modes of interaction with it (Barlas, 2024). One of the most striking shifts has been the transition of religious knowledge into the digital domain, especially following the onset of the COVID-19 pandemic. This transition offers a new epistemological framework that allows for broader engagement (Baker et al., 2020). Online platforms enable individuals to access religious content, participate in discussions, and challenge or redefine traditional religious authorities. This interactivity has opened avenues for diverse perspectives, empowering individuals to interpret religious teachings outside the confines of institutionalized control. Also, religious participation has evolved significantly in the digital age. Unlike traditional believers who practice their faith within the boundaries of a specific physical community, online users engage with religion more fluidly and interactively. Digital platforms facilitate forming religious communities that transcend geographical, cultural, and even doctrinal boundaries. Integrating digital media into religious experiences has further reshaped contemporary faith practices. Scholars such as Hutchings (2016) detailed how digital platforms, through hypermediation and hybrid spaces, transformed religious experiences, creating new avenues for believers to engage with their faith. This integration diversifies religious participation and enriches how individuals experience spirituality in the digital realm. The impact of technology on religious formation is another critical development in this digital transformation. The accessibility of diverse religious perspectives through online platforms has significantly influenced young people's belief systems. Many now seek spiritual guidance from social media sources, shifting religious authority's locus from traditional institutions to decentralized, user-generated interpretations. This trend underscores the increasing contestation of religious authority in the digital age, as individuals navigate a multitude of theological perspectives without necessarily adhering to a singular doctrine. The Impact of Digitalization on Religious Practices and Community Dynamics … Constantin GRIGORE & Alexandra COBZEANU 304 Moreover, the Internet has revolutionized the study of religion by altering how sacred texts and religious knowledge are accessed and interpreted. In contrast to traditional textual research methods, the digital age emphasizes visual and interactive approaches, making religious education more engaging and accessible to a broader audience. This shift has also contributed to diversifying religious learning experiences, allowing individuals to explore faith innovatively and personally. Despite these advancements, the digitalization of religion also presents significant challenges. Ethical, social, and theological concerns arise regarding the authenticity of online religious practices, the reliability of digital sources, and the nature of virtual religious communities. Questions regarding religious authority, community cohesion, and the legitimacy of digital sacraments remain debated among scholars and religious leaders alike. The rapid proliferation of online religious movements further complicates traditional understandings of faith and spiritual authority, necessitating ongoing scholarly inquiry into the implications of these transformations. Furthermore, emerging technologies, including artificial intelligence, have introduced new forms of religious participation, particularly among younger generations. AI-driven religious applications, virtual congregations, and automated theological discussions illustrate how technology continues to reshape faith-based experiences. These innovations highlight the evolving nature of spirituality in the digital era, where faith practices are increasingly mediated through technology. Nevertheless, the available literature on these topics, particularly following the onset of the COVID-19 pandemic, must be systematically reviewed to understand the profound changes better. The digital world and religious education Traditionally, religious education has been primarily studied within confessional contexts, receiving limited attention as a field of study and research in secular educational spaces. In Romania, few studies within the education sciences explore religion's academic potential in fostering transversal competencies, character formation, or supporting mental health. Instead, religion has often been at the center of educational policy controversies, particularly regarding its inclusion in the standard curriculum or designation as an optional subject. Additionally, numerous debates have emerged concerning the content of religious education, ranging from a strictly confessional approach to an interfaith presentation of religious concepts. The current global context underscores an urgent need to investigate and understand religious education within a new framework compared to previous discussions, i.e., the digital environment. One of the most significant Revista Românească pentru Educaţie Multidimensională April-June 2025 Volume 17, Issue 2 305 contexts in this regard is the COVID-19 pandemic and the global lockdown, which served as catalysts for digital transformation, including within the religious sphere (Dzirasa-Payne, 2023; Kołodziejska et al., 2024). Millions of individuals were compelled to access religious content and participate in online rituals, accelerating a developing trend. This abrupt transition has raised some important questions: How has the online environment influenced religious education? How have religious authorities adapted to the digital landscape? What new forms of religious community and belonging have emerged in virtual spaces? Without rigorous research, we risk overlooking profound transformations within the contemporary religious landscape. A deeper examination of this phenomenon is necessary to understand digital religious education's implications on individual faith development and communal religious experiences. The shift to online religious engagement has introduced new dynamics in learning, interaction, and authority structures within faith communities. Virtual platforms have facilitated access to religious knowledge and redefined how individuals connect with their faith, religious leaders, and fellow believers. Moreover, integrating technology into religious education presents opportunities and challenges. On the one hand, digital tools can enhance accessibility, allowing individuals from diverse backgrounds to engage with religious teachings flexibly and interactively. Online resources, virtual classrooms, and digital theological discussions provide new avenues for exploring faith-related topics beyond traditional institutional settings. On the other hand, concerns about authenticity, doctrinal accuracy, and the depth of spiritual engagement in digital contexts necessitate careful examination. The role of religious educators, the credibility of digital religious sources, and the potential for misinformation are critical aspects that must be addressed in ongoing research. The present study The present study aimed to examine the impact of digitalization on religious practices and community dynamics through a systematic review of the literature published after the beginning of the COVID-19 pandemic (2020-2024). This general aim was two-fold: first, we aimed to examine the digital shift in religious practices, more specifically to (1) Identify how religious rituals, worship, and community gatherings are adapted to digital and virtual platforms; (2) Explore changes in religious observance due to the use of online tools, social media, live streaming, and virtual reality, and (3) Investigate the role of digitalization in facilitating or hindering traditional religious practices. Second, we aimed to understand the role of technology in religious communities, more specifically to (4) Examine how digital platforms influence The Impact of Digitalization on Religious Practices and Community Dynamics … Constantin GRIGORE & Alexandra COBZEANU 306 religious socialization, community building, and interfaith interactions; (5) Analyze the role of digitalization in religious education; and (6) Investigate the ethical and theological implications of digital religious participation. The primary research question was: How do digitalization and virtual environments transform religious practices and experiences? Methodology Our research methodology uses a systematic literature review following the PRISMA-ScR guidelines (Liberati et. al 2009). This methodology is appropriate for synthesizing existing knowledge on the relationship between digitalization, online environments, religious practices, and community dynamics. It allows us to identify key trends, gaps in the literature, and potential areas for future research. Data analysis This review adhered to the PRISMA-ScR guidelines. We limited our search to peer-reviewed studies published in English that addressed our research topic within a four-year period (2020-2024), coinciding with the COVID-19 pandemic. Grey literature was excluded. In February 2025, we searched ERIC, JSTOR, Web of Science, and Scopus, refining our search strategy through collaborative discussions. Relevant articles were then exported to Google Sheets, where duplicates were removed. We used the following keywords from the available literature to refine our search strategy: ("religion" OR "religiousness" OR "religiosity") AND ("digital" OR "digitalization" OR" virtual" OR" online") AND "religious practices" AND "religious experiences". The search strategy was then applied to all the databases, and the authors evaluated titles, abstracts, and full articles for relevant publications according to the eligibility criteria. Disputes regarding the selection of publications were resolved through discussions and consensus. Data collection and data analysis methods A total of 115 articles were initially identified through a systematic search across the four databases (see Fig.1). Specifically, six articles were retrieved from ERIC, 25 from JSTOR, 42 from Web of Science (WOS), and 43 from SCOPUS. Following the initial collection, the next crucial step involved eliminating duplicate records to ensure each article was unique and not counted multiple times across databases. After this refinement, the number of articles remaining for further analysis was reduced to 100. The distribution after deduplication was as follows: 6 articles from ERIC, 25 from Revista Românească pentru Educaţie Multidimensională April-June 2025 Volume 17, Issue 2 307 JSTOR, 40 from WOS, and 29 from SCOPUS. This step was essential to avoid redundancy and maintain the integrity of the literature review. The PRISMA Statement comprises an item checklist and a four-phase flow diagram. The checklist contains items considered vital for the transparent reporting of a review. The diagram paper elucidates the significance and justification for each checklist item. Based on this preliminary assessment, the pool of articles was further narrowed down to 49, with three articles retained from ERIC, four from JSTOR, 31 from WOS, and 11 from SCOPUS. Subsequently, an abstract-level review was carried out to refine the selection further. By carefully evaluating the abstracts, 47 articles met the inclusion criteria and were deemed suitable for an in-depth investigation. This stage was particularly important in determining whether each study's scope, methodology, and findings aligned with the research objectives. Finally, the most important step involved a full-text review of the selected studies to assess the quality of research, depth of analysis, and relevance to the study. # The Impact of Digital Media on Religious Practices and Community Engagement

### Abstract

The rapid expansion of digital media has profoundly influenced religious education and identity formation, reshaping how individuals’ access, engage with, and express their religious beliefs. This study examines the transformative role of digital media in these areas, focusing on its impact on the accessibility and interactivity of religious education, as well as its role in the construction and expression of religious identity. Digital platforms, such as online courses, social media, and virtual communities, have democratized religious knowledge, enabling people from diverse backgrounds to engage with religious teachings and practices in flexible and personalized ways. Moreover, digital media has allowed for new forms of self-representation, facilitating the creation of individualized religious identities that blend various traditions and practices. Despite these advancements, the study also highlights the challenges posed by digital media, including the risk of misinformation, commercialization of religious content, and the fragmentation of communal religious practices. This paper concludes by discussing the need for a balanced approach to digital media in religious contexts, ensuring that its benefits are maximized while addressing its potential drawbacks to maintain the authenticity and integrity of religious expression and practice in the digital age.

Abstract
--------

Mediatization and digitalization are trends that are increasingly affecting religious communities and their communicative practices. While many aspects of these developments have been described theoretically and empirically, little is known about the dynamic interplay between digital media use, an individual’s religious meaning system, and the relationships within a religious community. Building on the theory of the mediatization of religion, the functionalist perspective of religion, media selection, and co-orientation research, we propose a dynamic model of digital media use in religious communities. Hereby, the religious functions of meaning-making and social connection are considered important drivers for how individuals engage with others. Additionally, theories on media selection help our understanding of the acceptance and domestication of new technologies, as well as selective exposure to specific content. In combination, the model links the individuals with the social context of their religious communities and vice versa. Furthermore, the theoretical model helps to combine and systematize empirical research from different disciplines that are relevant to understanding today’s digital religious media use. We therefore conclude with a discussion of the benefits of the model for future theoretical developments and empirical research in the field of digital religion and beyond.

Keywords: 

[digital media](https://www.mdpi.com/search?q=digital+media); [religious community](https://www.mdpi.com/search?q=religious+community); [meaning](https://www.mdpi.com/search?q=meaning); [social connection](https://www.mdpi.com/search?q=social+connection); [mediatization](https://www.mdpi.com/search?q=mediatization); [functional perspective](https://www.mdpi.com/search?q=functional+perspective); [co-orientation](https://www.mdpi.com/search?q=co-orientation)

1. Introduction
---------------

Belonging to a religious community is a central aspect of religious life ([Durkheim 1995](https://www.mdpi.com/2077-1444/15/7/762#B17-religions-15-00762); [Lundby 2011](https://www.mdpi.com/2077-1444/15/7/762#B61-religions-15-00762)). In its broadest sense, a **religious community** can be defined as a set of people sharing specific religious beliefs and practices (e.g., Christianity, Judaism, or Islam). Such a community does not necessarily require a formal organization ([Durkheim 1995](https://www.mdpi.com/2077-1444/15/7/762#B17-religions-15-00762)) and may range in size from small groups, whose members are in direct contact (e.g., prayer groups in a Christian church), to large numbers of people who are only indirectly linked by these shared practices and beliefs (e.g., whole congregations) ([Everton 2018](https://www.mdpi.com/2077-1444/15/7/762#B22-religions-15-00762)). Being in direct or indirect contact implies that all individuals within a religious community can influence one another and, at the same time, define the characteristics of the community. More formally, we can state that individuals and the community they are part of are mutually dependent on each other ([Friemel 2021](https://www.mdpi.com/2077-1444/15/7/762#B30-religions-15-00762); [Newcomb 1953](https://www.mdpi.com/2077-1444/15/7/762#B68-religions-15-00762)). Given that we build on a rather inclusive definition of communities that may range from small groups to whole religions, we do not distinguish between a meso and macro level. Nevertheless, the proposed model of media use in religious communities can be considered a holistic approach, to overcome the lack of integration of different levels of analysis ([Lövheim and Hjarvard 2019](https://www.mdpi.com/2077-1444/15/7/762#B58-religions-15-00762)). 

Considering religious individuals as social actors inside religious communities further helps to understand the dynamic between two important constructs, a religious meaning system and social relationships. Both are central to understanding religion and have received increasing attention in the digital age ([Campbell and Sheldon 2022](https://www.mdpi.com/2077-1444/15/7/762#B9-religions-15-00762); [Park 2005](https://www.mdpi.com/2077-1444/15/7/762#B73-religions-15-00762); [Hoover 2006](https://www.mdpi.com/2077-1444/15/7/762#B42-religions-15-00762)). In this article, we approach religious meaning systems and social relationships from a **functionalist perspective on religion**.[1](https://www.mdpi.com/2077-1444/15/7/762#fn001-religions-15-00762) This means that we understand religion through the functions it can fulfill ([Batson and Stocks 2004](https://www.mdpi.com/2077-1444/15/7/762#B3-religions-15-00762); [Knoblauch 1999](https://www.mdpi.com/2077-1444/15/7/762#B53-religions-15-00762); [Luckmann 1991](https://www.mdpi.com/2077-1444/15/7/762#B59-religions-15-00762); [Durkheim 1995](https://www.mdpi.com/2077-1444/15/7/762#B17-religions-15-00762)). In other words, religion can help shape and maintain a meaning system through the function of **religious meaning-making** ([Batson and Stocks 2004](https://www.mdpi.com/2077-1444/15/7/762#B3-religions-15-00762); [Park et al. 2013](https://www.mdpi.com/2077-1444/15/7/762#B75-religions-15-00762); [Schnell 2011](https://www.mdpi.com/2077-1444/15/7/762#B82-religions-15-00762)). At the same time, religion forms and maintains relationships through the function of **religious social connection** ([Pollack 2017](https://www.mdpi.com/2077-1444/15/7/762#B78-religions-15-00762); [Knoblauch 1999](https://www.mdpi.com/2077-1444/15/7/762#B53-religions-15-00762); [Homann 1997](https://www.mdpi.com/2077-1444/15/7/762#B41-religions-15-00762)). It is important to note that the two functions are interdependent. On the one hand, relationships can make meaning and inform an individual’s meaning system, thereby inducing religious meaning-making ([Taves 2018](https://www.mdpi.com/2077-1444/15/7/762#B86-religions-15-00762); [Wong and Fry 1998](https://www.mdpi.com/2077-1444/15/7/762#B92-religions-15-00762); [Park 2005](https://www.mdpi.com/2077-1444/15/7/762#B73-religions-15-00762); [Zarzycka et al. 2020](https://www.mdpi.com/2077-1444/15/7/762#B94-religions-15-00762)). On the other hand, similar religious meaning systems can support social connections, shared beliefs, and attitudes, resulting in the formation of relationships ([Newcomb 1953](https://www.mdpi.com/2077-1444/15/7/762#B68-religions-15-00762); [Oh 2020](https://www.mdpi.com/2077-1444/15/7/762#B71-religions-15-00762); [Lazarsfeld and Merton 1954](https://www.mdpi.com/2077-1444/15/7/762#B55-religions-15-00762); [Saroglou 2011](https://www.mdpi.com/2077-1444/15/7/762#B81-religions-15-00762); [Knoblauch 1999](https://www.mdpi.com/2077-1444/15/7/762#B53-religions-15-00762)). While the literature also refers to other functions of religion ([Knoblauch 1999](https://www.mdpi.com/2077-1444/15/7/762#B53-religions-15-00762)), these two seem to be central for understanding dynamics within a religious community in the digital age ([Hjarvard 2008b](https://www.mdpi.com/2077-1444/15/7/762#B37-religions-15-00762); [Hoover and Park 2004](https://www.mdpi.com/2077-1444/15/7/762#B43-religions-15-00762); [Hoover 2006](https://www.mdpi.com/2077-1444/15/7/762#B42-religions-15-00762); [Campbell and Sheldon 2022](https://www.mdpi.com/2077-1444/15/7/762#B9-religions-15-00762)).

Over the past several decades, **digitalization** has become an issue of increasing relevance for all parts of society, including the religious and spiritual spheres ([Tsuria and Campbell 2022](https://www.mdpi.com/2077-1444/15/7/762#B87-religions-15-00762)). Believers use digital media for religious or spiritual practices (e.g., prayer apps; [Campbell et al. 2014](https://www.mdpi.com/2077-1444/15/7/762#B10-religions-15-00762)), to get information about certain aspects of their religion ([Brubaker and Haigh 2017](https://www.mdpi.com/2077-1444/15/7/762#B4-religions-15-00762)), and to connect online ([Hutchings 2013](https://www.mdpi.com/2077-1444/15/7/762#B46-religions-15-00762)). The increasing relevance of digital media and its impact on religion, religious communities, and individuals can be seen as a continuation of the longer-lasting **mediatization of religion** ([Lövheim and Hjarvard 2019](https://www.mdpi.com/2077-1444/15/7/762#B58-religions-15-00762); [Hjarvard 2008a](https://www.mdpi.com/2077-1444/15/7/762#B36-religions-15-00762)). Mediatization, in general, describes a process by which media institutions (i.e., their logic and rules) influence other societal institutions, such as politics, family, or religion ([Hjarvard 2008b](https://www.mdpi.com/2077-1444/15/7/762#B37-religions-15-00762); [Couldry and Hepp 2013](https://www.mdpi.com/2077-1444/15/7/762#B13-religions-15-00762)). While mediatization originally referred to classic mass media (e.g., print, radio, and TV), it now also includes digital media ([Finnemann 2014](https://www.mdpi.com/2077-1444/15/7/762#B24-religions-15-00762)). Digital media can include, but are not limited to, digital visuals, digital audio, websites, and online games ([Helland 2016](https://www.mdpi.com/2077-1444/15/7/762#B35-religions-15-00762); [Grieve 2022](https://www.mdpi.com/2077-1444/15/7/762#B31-religions-15-00762)). The mediatization of religion hence describes a process “through which religious beliefs, agency, and symbols are becoming influenced by the workings of various media” ([Hjarvard 2016, p. 8](https://www.mdpi.com/2077-1444/15/7/762#B39-religions-15-00762)) and has been discussed with respect to traditional media, such as television (TV), radio, and print ([Christensen 2012](https://www.mdpi.com/2077-1444/15/7/762#B12-religions-15-00762); [Hjarvard 2008a](https://www.mdpi.com/2077-1444/15/7/762#B36-religions-15-00762)), as well as digital media, such as websites and social media ([Fischer-Nielsen 2012](https://www.mdpi.com/2077-1444/15/7/762#B25-religions-15-00762)). Empirical studies on mediatization have investigated the role specific (digital) media can play in the life of believers, such as internet communication ([Fischer-Nielsen 2012](https://www.mdpi.com/2077-1444/15/7/762#B25-religions-15-00762)) or the radio ([Setianto 2015](https://www.mdpi.com/2077-1444/15/7/762#B83-religions-15-00762)), how digital media can impact whole religious communities ([Nwankwo 2022](https://www.mdpi.com/2077-1444/15/7/762#B70-religions-15-00762)), how faith is communicated online ([Pavić et al. 2018](https://www.mdpi.com/2077-1444/15/7/762#B76-religions-15-00762)), and how certain online movements, such as fandoms, can have similarities to traditional religious institutions ([Petersen 2010](https://www.mdpi.com/2077-1444/15/7/762#B77-religions-15-00762)).

A result of this mediatization is that the functions of religious meaning-making and social connection may also be fulfilled through the use of media ([Lövheim and Hjarvard 2019](https://www.mdpi.com/2077-1444/15/7/762#B58-religions-15-00762); [Hjarvard 2008a](https://www.mdpi.com/2077-1444/15/7/762#B36-religions-15-00762); [Hoover and Park 2004](https://www.mdpi.com/2077-1444/15/7/762#B43-religions-15-00762); [Campbell and Sheldon 2022](https://www.mdpi.com/2077-1444/15/7/762#B9-religions-15-00762)). This represents a historical change because, for centuries, the functions of religious meaning-making and social connection were exclusively attached to religious organizations, their physical presence (e.g., churches), and oral communication ([Kaden and Schnettler 2020](https://www.mdpi.com/2077-1444/15/7/762#B50-religions-15-00762); [van der Merwe 2010](https://www.mdpi.com/2077-1444/15/7/762#B90-religions-15-00762); [Hjarvard 2008a](https://www.mdpi.com/2077-1444/15/7/762#B36-religions-15-00762)). Today, digital media provide new means to fulfill the function of religious meaning-making and, in turn, influence an individual’s religious meaning system ([Hoover 2006](https://www.mdpi.com/2077-1444/15/7/762#B42-religions-15-00762)) and allow an extension of the function of social connection beyond the local community into the digital sphere, thereby creating and shaping relationships ([Hjarvard 2008a](https://www.mdpi.com/2077-1444/15/7/762#B36-religions-15-00762); [Hutchings 2011](https://www.mdpi.com/2077-1444/15/7/762#B45-religions-15-00762), [2013](https://www.mdpi.com/2077-1444/15/7/762#B46-religions-15-00762); [Campbell and Sheldon 2022](https://www.mdpi.com/2077-1444/15/7/762#B9-religions-15-00762)).

enriched_points.expanded_content: Digital platforms have transformed traditional religious communities, enabling new forms of connection that prioritize individual preferences, resulting in a more fluid and networked understanding of community.

Digital engagement allows individuals to explore diverse religious perspectives, facilitating a dynamic and hybrid identity that blends various traditions and personal beliefs beyond traditional boundaries.

The accessibility of online resources fosters an environment where individuals can creatively combine elements from different faiths, leading to personalized spiritual practices that reflect individual beliefs and experiences.
